macro_segments.title: What Is Not Sold with a Field: Wells, Presses, and Pigeon Coops

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Items excluded from field sales
Access rights to retained items: Rabbi Akiva vs. the Sages
The flip case: When the well is sold but the field is retained
When the exclusion applies: The בעין רעה principle
Gifts include all items, including separately-conceived objects

macro_segments.title: Brothers Dividing an Inherited Field: Full Ownership Transfer

micro_segments.title: Principle: Complete separation when brothers divide an estate
Why inheritance division differs from sales and gifts

macro_segments.title: Possession of a Stranger's Property: Chazaka and Hefker Land

micro_segments.title: Taking possession of a whole field through חזקה
How chazaka differs from sales despite both including trees
The Rashbam's explanation: Separate fields without a boundary line

macro_segments.title: Consecration to the Temple: Rav Shimon's Limitation

micro_segments.title: Full consecration versus limited consecration of field components
Rav Shimon: Only trees are included in consecration, not wells

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Explanation: Sale vs. Gift and the Power of Silence

micro_segments.title: Yehuda ben Nakusa's cryptic explanation and the Gemara's challenge
The proper answer: Buyer's duty to clarify in sales; gift receiver's silence
Combining בעין יפה with the inability to request clarification

macro_segments.title: The House Case: Generous Interpretation of Deathbed Gifts

micro_segments.title: A house described by barrel capacity: Determining the actual gift
Rav Ashi's principle: Always interpret gifts in the most generous way

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Explanation: Sale vs. Gift and the Power of Silence (continued)

micro_segments.title: Counterbalance: Voiding gifts when assumptions prove false

macro_segments.title: Hekdesh and Trees: Why Only Trees Are Included Despite Limited Scope

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's distinction: Buying trees vs. retaining trees for oneself
Why trees drawing nutrients require land rights even for Rabbi Akiva
Comparison with wells: Wells don't draw on land; trees do

macro_segments.title: Rav Shimon's Model: Hekdesh as a Sale, Not a Gift

micro_segments.title: Rav Shimon's reasoning: Avoiding me'ilah when trees draw from holy land
The Gemara's challenge: How can you retain the tree without rights?
Resolution: Rav Shimon follows Rabbi Akiva's model for hekdesh
The final point: Rav Huna's חידוש on retained land rights

topical_tags.term: Excluded from field sale (well, press, pigeon coop)
Rabbi Akiva vs. Sages on access rights (בעין יפה vs. בעין רעה)
Gift (מתנה) includes all items, even separate objects
Inheritance division (חלוקת אחים) grants full ownership
Possession (חזקה) of hefker land includes all items
Consecration (הקדש) differs from sale and gift
Rav Shimon: Trees included in hekdesh; well not included
Buyer's duty to clarify terms (היה לו לפרש)
Silence in gift-giving: receiver cannot ask for clarification
Voiding gifts based on false assumptions
Me'ilah: benefiting improperly from holy property
Rav Huna's principle: retaining tree implies land rights
